title: Listening - Part 3: Nature’s Clean-up Crew
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with an environmental scientist, Noah, and a biotechnology entrepreneur, Sonia, about using living organisms to remove pollution. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview exploring 'oil-eating' bacteria, the use of mushrooms to absorb heavy metals, and the ethical debate over 'designer' microbes.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Traditional pollution clean-up usually involves digging up dirt and burning it. But what if we could use nature to heal itself? Noah, what exactly is 'biorremediation'?

Noah: [15] It’s essentially using life—bacteria, fungi, or plants—to neutralize contaminants. [/15] For example, after a major oil spill, there are certain microbes that naturally 'eat' hydrocarbons. We don't bring in chemicals; we just provide these bacteria with enough nitrogen and oxygen to let them thrive and finish their meal. It’s cleaner, cheaper, and far less invasive than mechanical dredging.

Interviewer: Sonia, your company works with 'mycoremediation.' That’s using mushrooms, right?

Sonia: [16] Exactly. Fungi are the planet’s great decomposers. Their root systems, or mycelium, produce enzymes that can break down plastic or even absorb heavy metals like lead from the soil. [/16] The mushrooms then store the toxins in their 'fruiting bodies.' We then harvest the mushrooms and dispose of them safely, effectively 'vacuuming' the toxins out of the earth.

Interviewer: Noah, this sounds perfect. Why isn't it the standard for every toxic site?

Noah: [17] The main issue is 'time.' While a bulldozer can clear a site in a week, biorremediation can take months or even years. [/17] In a world of fast real-estate development, people aren't always willing to wait. Also, if the conditions aren't exactly right—the temperature or the pH—the microbes simply stop working.

Interviewer: Sonia, what about using 'genetically modified' organisms to speed it up?

Sonia: [18] That’s the cutting edge. We can engineer 'designer' microbes that are much more efficient. But it creates a massive ethical debate. [/18] If we release a synthetic bacteria into the wild to clean up a spill, we don't know if it might eventually outcompete native species and disrupt the local ecosystem. It’s a 'Frankenstein's monster' scenario that we have to be very careful with.

Interviewer: Is there a risk that the pollutants just move up the food chain?

Noah: [19] That is a concern with 'phytoremediation'—using plants. If a sunflower absorbs lead and then a deer eats that sunflower, the poison has just changed address. [/19] You have to manage the site carefully and ensure the organisms are removed before they can be eaten by local wildlife.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Sonia? Is this the future of environmentalism?

Sonia: [20] It has to be. We have created too much waste for humans to clean up alone. We need to partner with the biological systems that have been recycling this planet for billions of years. [/20]

Interviewer: Noah, Sonia, thank you.

questions.question: According to Noah, how do bacteria help after an oil spill?
options: A) They produce chemicals that make the oil easier to burn.
B) They physically move the oil to a different location.
C) They consume the hydrocarbons present in the oil.
D) They prevent the oil from reaching the surface of the water.
questions.explanation: He says certain microbes naturally 'eat' hydrocarbons and neutralise the oil.

questions.question: What happens to heavy metals during 'mycoremediation'?
options: A) They are turned into a safe gas by the mushrooms.
B) They are absorbed and stored in the body of the mushroom.
C) They are pushed deeper into the ground away from plants.
D) They are used as a source of energy for the mycelium.
questions.explanation: Sonia explains that mushrooms store toxins in their 'fruiting bodies', which are then safely harvested.

questions.question: What is the primary reason why biorremediation is not always used?
options: A) It is much more expensive than using heavy machinery.
B) The process takes a lot longer than traditional methods.
C) It requires the use of very dangerous chemicals.
D) It only works on sites where no people live.
questions.explanation: Noah mentions that while a bulldozer takes a week, this process can take months or years.

questions.question: Sonia warns that 'designer' microbes could
options: A) be stolen by rival biotechnology companies.
B) fail to survive in the cold temperature of the soil.
C) negatively impact the natural balance of local ecosystems.
D) cause humans to develop new types of diseases.
questions.explanation: She mentions they might outcompete native species and refers to a 'Frankenstein's monster' scenario.

questions.question: Noah mentions the 'deer' example to illustrate
options: A) how wildlife can help humans clean up the environment.
B) the risk of toxins being transferred from plants to animals.
C) the fact that most animals are immune to lead poisoning.
D) why sunflowers are the best plant for purifying water.
questions.explanation: He explains that if a deer eats a lead-filled sunflower, the poison has just 'changed address'.

questions.question: In her conclusion, Sonia argues that
options: A) humans are now capable of cleaning the planet without help.
B) we must work together with existing biological processes.
C) we should stop all industrial production to prevent waste.
D) technology will eventually make all bacteria unnecessary.
questions.explanation: She states that we need to 'partner with the biological systems' that recycle the planet.
